questions.scenario:
A new bastion was built from an old image. The active SSH settings are:

```text
Port 22
PermitRootLogin yes
PasswordAuthentication yes
PubkeyAuthentication yes
MaxAuthTries 6
PermitEmptyPasswords no
```

Which remediation items match the article's hardening target?

options.text: Set `PasswordAuthentication no` after confirming key-based login works
Set `PermitRootLogin no` so root cannot log in directly
Move SSH away from port 22, for example `Port 2222`, to reduce scanner noise
Set `PubkeyAuthentication no` so passwords become the only supported login method
Lower `MaxAuthTries` to a small value such as `3`

questions.explanation: The hardening section recommends key-based auth, no direct root login, a non-default port to reduce noise, and fewer authentication attempts. Disabling public-key authentication would undermine key-based access and make password exposure worse.

questions.scenario:
A scanner finds these two differences between production and the approved bastion baseline:

```text
production: PasswordAuthentication yes
baseline:   PasswordAuthentication no

production: PrintMotd no
baseline:   PrintMotd yes
```

Which finding is the highest-risk authentication exposure?

options.text: `PasswordAuthentication yes`, because an internet-facing bastion will accept brute-force password attempts
`PrintMotd no`, because missing a login banner prevents TCP encryption
`PrintMotd no`, because it grants root access to unauthenticated clients
`PasswordAuthentication yes`, but only because it disables SSH logging entirely

questions.explanation: Password authentication is a direct attack surface for brute-force login attempts. A message-of-the-day setting may be a policy issue, but it is not the same immediate authentication exposure.

questions.scenario: A change request sets `PasswordAuthentication no` on a cloud instance. The requester has not tested SSH key login yet and the instance has no serial console. What should the reviewer require before approval?

options.text: Verify that an authorized key is installed and a key-based login succeeds before disabling passwords
Disable `PubkeyAuthentication` first so the password change is easier to observe
Apply the change immediately because password removal can always be fixed through DNS
Remove `AllowUsers` because user restrictions prevent the kernel from reading SSH keys

questions.explanation: The article warns that disabling passwords before key-based access works can lock you out of a cloud instance. The safe rollout proves key login first, then disables password authentication.

questions.scenario: A bastion owner argues that changing `Port 22` to `Port 2222` is enough hardening by itself. What is the best response?

options.text: A non-default port reduces automated scanner noise, but meaningful hardening still requires controls such as key-only auth and no root login
Changing the port encrypts SSH traffic, so password authentication becomes safe on the internet
Changing the port disables root login automatically in OpenSSH
Port changes are harmful because SSH can only run on TCP 22

questions.explanation: The article explicitly calls a port change obscurity, not real security. It helps reduce log noise, but it must be combined with stronger authentication and authorization settings.

questions.scenario:
The active config says:

```text
PermitRootLogin yes
AllowUsers deploy admin
```

Why is `PermitRootLogin yes` still a hardening violation?

options.text: Direct root login removes the extra barrier of compromising a regular user first and then escalating
Root login forces SSH to use UDP instead of TCP
Root login disables `AllowUsers` for every account on the machine
Root login is required only when password authentication is disabled

questions.explanation: `PermitRootLogin no` prevents direct remote access to the most privileged account. `AllowUsers` is useful, but it does not make direct root login a good default.

questions.scenario: An audit discovers a contractor account that still exists in `/etc/passwd`. The SSH config has no `AllowUsers` line. Which hardening change best reduces the risk that this forgotten account can SSH in?

options.text: Add an `AllowUsers` list containing only the approved operator accounts
Raise `MaxAuthTries` so legitimate users have more attempts
Set `PermitEmptyPasswords yes` so account state is easier to test
Remove `PubkeyAuthentication yes` so user allowlisting happens automatically

questions.explanation: The article describes `AllowUsers` as a way to restrict SSH access to named accounts. A forgotten system user should not become a remote login path unless it is explicitly approved.

questions.scenario:
A hardened baseline includes:

```text
PasswordAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
KexAlgorithms curve25519-sha256,curve25519-sha256@libssh.org
```

A proposed rollback changes only `PubkeyAuthentication yes` to `PubkeyAuthentication no`. What is the operational impact?

options.text: Key-based login would be disabled, which conflicts with the baseline that also disables password login
Only the SSH port would change, so existing key users would not notice
The change would improve hardening because passwords and keys should both be disabled for SSH
The change affects only DNS resolution for the bastion hostname

questions.explanation: The baseline depends on public-key authentication because password authentication is disabled. Turning off public-key auth can lock out legitimate users and does not match the key-based-only hardening goal.
